signature_dish.name: Old Monk Dal
signature_dish.description: Adda's legendary black lentil dal, slow-cooked for 24 hours until impossibly creamy. Named after the beloved Indian rum, it's rich, smoky, and the dish that put this spot on every best-of list.
ingredients: 1 cup whole black urad dal (soaked overnight)
instructions: Soak dal overnight, then drain and rinse.
In a heavy pot or pressure cooker, cover dal with 4 cups water. Cook until completely soft and breaking apart (1 hour simmering or 30 min in pressure cooker).
In a separate pan, heat 2 tbsp butter. Add cumin seeds and let them crackle.
Add onion and cook until deeply golden, about 12 minutes.
Add garlic and ginger, cook 2 minutes.
Add tomatoes, chili powder, turmeric, and garam masala. Cook until oil separates, about 8 minutes.
Add this masala to the cooked dal. Stir well and simmer on lowest heat for 30+ minutes, stirring occasionally.
Finish with remaining butter, cream, and salt. The longer it simmers, the better it gets.
Garnish with cilantro and a swirl of cream.
pro_tips: The secret is TIME — real dal makhani simmers for hours. Weekend project.
Use a slow cooker on low for 8 hours for the most authentic texture.
A pinch of dried fenugreek leaves (kasuri methi) at the end is the restaurant secret.

signature_dish.name: Jollof Rice with Suya-Spiced Prawns
signature_dish.description: Chef Kwame Onwuachi's stunning take on West African jollof rice, topped with suya-spiced grilled prawns. The dish that earned Tatiana a James Beard Award.
ingredients: 1 lb large prawns, peeled and deveined
instructions: Blend tomatoes, bell pepper, half the onion, garlic, and ginger into a smooth paste.
Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pot. Fry remaining onion until golden.
Add tomato paste and cook 3 minutes until darkened.
Pour in the blended tomato mixture. Cook on medium-high for 20 minutes, stirring often, until reduced and oil floats on top.
Add stock, smoked paprika, and the whole habanero. Bring to a boil.
Add rinsed rice, stir once, cover tightly. Cook on lowest heat for 25-30 minutes.
For suya spice: mix ground peanuts, garlic powder, onion powder, cayenne, coriander, and salt.
Coat prawns in suya spice. Grill or sear in a hot pan, 2 minutes per side.
Fluff rice, remove habanero. Serve topped with suya prawns.
pro_tips: Don't stir the rice after the initial stir — you want the smoky bottom layer (the 'party jollof' crust).
Toast the suya spice mix in a dry pan before coating the prawns for deeper flavor.
If you can find scotch bonnet peppers, use those instead of habanero for authenticity.

signature_dish.name: Bone-In Prime Rib with Dripping Fries
signature_dish.description: London's legendary steakhouse brought their signature dry-aged prime rib to NYC. Cooked over charcoal, served with beef-dripping triple-cooked fries that are worth the trip alone.
ingredients: Beef drippings or tallow (¼ cup for fries)
instructions: Remove prime rib from fridge 2 hours before cooking. Season generously with salt and pepper.
Preheat oven to 250°F. Place rib on a rack in a roasting pan.
Roast until internal temp reaches 120°F for medium-rare, about 3-4 hours.
Rest for 30 minutes, tented with foil.
For triple-cooked fries: cut potatoes into thick chips. Simmer in salted water until just tender (10 min). Drain and cool completely.
Fry at 300°F for 5 minutes until pale and cooked through. Cool again.
Final fry at 375°F until golden and crispy, 3-4 minutes.
Toss hot fries with melted beef drippings and flaky salt.
Mix crème fraîche, grated horseradish, and mustard for sauce.
Sear the rested prime rib in a screaming hot cast iron pan for the crust.
pro_tips: The triple-cook method is non-negotiable — it's what makes these fries shatteringly crispy.
If you can get beef tallow, use it for the final fry instead of vegetable oil.
Dry-aging at home: salt the roast uncovered in the fridge for 3-4 days before cooking.

signature_dish.name: Burrata Pizza
signature_dish.description: L'Industrie's most Instagrammed item — a perfect thin-crust pizza topped with a whole ball of burrata, cherry tomatoes, fresh basil, and Mike's Hot Honey. Simple, outrageous, iconic.
ingredients: ½ cup San Marzano tomato sauce
instructions: Preheat oven as hot as it goes (500-550°F) with a pizza stone or steel inside for at least 1 hour.
Stretch dough into a 12-inch round on a semolina-dusted surface.
Spread a thin layer of tomato sauce, leaving a 1-inch border.
Scatter cherry tomato halves over the sauce.
Transfer to the hot stone. Bake 8-12 minutes until crust is charred and bubbly.
Immediately place the whole burrata in the center of the hot pizza.
Drizzle generously with hot honey and olive oil.
Tear basil leaves over the top. Finish with flaky salt.
Cut and serve immediately — the burrata should ooze when you slice through it.
pro_tips: Let the burrata come to room temperature before placing on the pizza.
A pizza steel gets hotter than a stone and gives better char.
The hot honey is essential — sweet, spicy, and creamy from the burrata is the magic trio.

signature_dish.name: Chrysanthemum Lasagna
signature_dish.description: Don Angie's show-stopping pinwheel lasagna, sliced crosswise to reveal a chrysanthemum flower pattern. A visual masterpiece that also happens to taste incredible — layers of pasta, ricotta, mozzarella, and a slow-cooked meat sauce.
instructions: Make meat sauce: brown beef and pork in olive oil. Add onion, garlic, tomato paste. Deglaze with wine. Add tomatoes, simmer 1 hour.
Mix ricotta with egg, half the parmesan, salt, pepper, and chopped basil.
Lay out lasagna sheets. Spread thin layer of ricotta mixture, then meat sauce, then mozzarella.
Roll each sheet up tightly into a cylinder.
Slice each roll into 1.5-inch pinwheels.
Spread a thin layer of meat sauce in a round baking dish.
Arrange pinwheels cut-side up, packed tightly in concentric circles (this creates the chrysanthemum pattern).
Spoon remaining sauce between the pinwheels. Top with remaining mozzarella and parmesan.
Bake at 375°F covered for 30 minutes, then uncovered for 15 minutes until bubbly and golden.
Let rest 10 minutes before serving.
pro_tips: Use a round dish — the concentric circles of pinwheels are what creates the flower pattern.
Freeze the rolls for 20 minutes before slicing for cleaner cuts.
Fresh pasta sheets work best — dried won't roll properly.

signature_dish.name: Champaran Meat
signature_dish.description: Dhamaka's most talked-about dish — a fiery Bihar-style mutton curry cooked sealed in a clay pot (handi) with nothing but meat, mustard oil, and an avalanche of chilis. No water, no tomatoes. Just pure, intense flavor.
ingredients: 2 lbs bone-in lamb or goat shoulder, cut into chunks
15 dried red chilis (Kashmiri for color, some Thai for heat)
instructions: Heat mustard oil in a heavy pot until it smokes, then cool slightly (this removes the raw flavor).
Add whole spices — cumin, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon — and let them crackle.
Add dried red chilis (break a few open for more heat) and toast 30 seconds.
Add onions and cook until deeply browned, 15 minutes.
Add garlic and ginger, cook 3 minutes.
Add lamb pieces, sear on high heat until browned all over.
Lower heat. Add yogurt one spoonful at a time, stirring between each addition.
Season with salt. The meat will cook in its own juices and the yogurt.
Cover tightly (traditionally sealed with dough). Cook on lowest heat for 2-3 hours.
Do not open the lid until done. The steam does all the work.
The result should be fall-off-the-bone tender with a concentrated, fiery sauce.
pro_tips: Goat is traditional and has more flavor than lamb, but lamb shoulder works great.
The sealed lid is key — if your pot doesn't seal well, wrap the lid junction in foil.
Serve with plain rice or thick rotis to balance the heat. This is NOT a mild dish.

signature_dish.name: Whole Butterflied Trout with Herbs and Brown Butter
signature_dish.description: Theodora's stunning whole trout, butterflied and pan-roasted until the skin is shatteringly crispy, finished with nutty brown butter, capers, and a shower of fresh herbs.
ingredients: Flaky sea salt and black pepper
instructions: Pat trout completely dry. Season flesh side with salt and pepper. Dust skin side lightly with flour.
Heat a large skillet over medium-high. Add olive oil.
Place trout skin-side down. Press gently with a spatula for the first minute to prevent curling.
Cook without moving for 4-5 minutes until skin is deeply golden and crispy.
Carefully flip. Cook flesh side just 1-2 minutes.
Transfer to plates skin-side up.
In the same pan, add butter. Cook over medium heat, swirling, until it foams and turns nutty brown (about 2 minutes).
Add capers (they'll splatter — be ready), garlic slices, and lemon juice.
Pour brown butter over the fish. Shower with herbs and flaky salt.
pro_tips: Dry fish = crispy skin. Pat it aggressively with paper towels.
The flour dust is thin — just enough to help the skin crisp, not a coating.
Don't rush the brown butter — it goes from brown to burnt in seconds. Watch the color and pull it off heat.

signature_dish.name: Khao Soi
signature_dish.description: Thai Diner's rich Northern Thai coconut curry noodle soup — a golden, fragrant broth loaded with egg noodles, tender chicken, crispy fried noodles on top, and pickled mustard greens on the side.
ingredients: 1 tbsp palm sugar or brown sugar
instructions: Heat 2 tbsp oil in a pot. Fry curry paste, curry powder, and turmeric for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add one can of coconut milk (the thick part). Stir and cook 5 minutes until oil separates.
Add chicken thighs, remaining coconut milk, and stock. Simmer 20 minutes until chicken is cooked.
Remove chicken, shred it, return to pot.
Season with fish sauce and sugar. Adjust to taste — it should be rich, slightly sweet, slightly salty.
Cook egg noodles according to package. Reserve a handful of dry noodles.
Fry the reserved dry noodles in hot oil until puffed and crispy (30 seconds).
Fry shallots until golden and crispy.
Serve noodles in bowls, ladle curry over top. Garnish with crispy noodles, fried shallots, pickled greens, cilantro, and lime.
pro_tips: Use Mae Ploy or Maesri red curry paste for the most authentic flavor.
The crispy noodle topping is mandatory — it adds essential texture contrast.
Make it a day ahead if you can — the flavors deepen overnight.

signature_dish.name: Lamb Kebab with Charred Eggplant
signature_dish.description: Laser Wolf's perfectly spiced lamb kebabs grilled over charcoal, served alongside their smoky, silky charred eggplant with tahini. Michael Solomonov's shipudiya (skewer house) concept at its best.
ingredients: 1 small onion, grated and squeezed dry
Fresh parsley and sumac for garnish
instructions: Mix lamb with grated onion, garlic, cumin, coriander, cinnamon, allspice, cayenne, salt, and pepper. Rest 30 minutes.
Shape into logs around flat metal skewers (or make oval patties if no skewers).
Grill over high heat (charcoal preferred), 3-4 minutes per side.
For eggplant: char whole eggplants directly on gas burner flame or under broiler, turning often, until completely collapsed and skin is blackened (15 min).
Scoop out flesh, discard skin. Drain excess liquid.
Chop eggplant roughly. Mix with tahini, lemon juice, garlic, salt, and olive oil.
Spread eggplant on plates, top with kebabs.
Drizzle with olive oil, sprinkle with sumac and parsley. Serve with warm pita.
pro_tips: Grate and SQUEEZE the onion dry — wet onion makes the kebab fall apart.
The eggplant must be truly charred — burnt skin = smoky flesh. Don't be timid.
Rest the meat mixture before shaping — it binds better when cold.

signature_dish.name: Insalata Verde
signature_dish.description: Via Carota's legendary green salad — deceptively simple, impossibly addictive. A mix of tender greens, fresh herbs, shaved vegetables, and a bright lemon vinaigrette. The salad that made people wait 2 hours for a table.
ingredients: 4 cups mixed tender greens (butter lettuce, frisée, arugula)
1 cup fresh herb leaves (parsley, dill, mint, basil — mix what you have)
2 celery stalks, shaved with a peeler
¼ cup extra-virgin olive oil (the best you have)
Flaky sea salt and fresh black pepper
instructions: Wash and thoroughly dry all greens and herbs. Cold, dry greens are essential.
Shave fennel and celery paper-thin using a mandoline or sharp peeler.
Make vinaigrette: whisk lemon juice, mustard, salt, and pepper. Slowly drizzle in olive oil while whisking.
In a large bowl, gently toss greens, herbs, fennel, celery, and cucumber.
Dress lightly — add vinaigrette a little at a time, tossing gently. You want every leaf kissed, not drowned.
Finish with flaky salt, cracked pepper, and shaved parm if using.
Serve immediately on a chilled plate.
pro_tips: The secret is the QUALITY of ingredients — this salad has nowhere to hide. Use the best olive oil and freshest greens you can find.
Chill your serving plates — a cold salad on a cold plate stays crisp longer.
Via Carota uses about 50/50 greens to herbs. Don't skimp on the herbs — they ARE the flavor.

signature_dish.name: Pepper Shrimp with Hibiscus Oil
signature_dish.description: Kabawa's showstopper — royal red shrimp blushing with dried hibiscus and bright orange pepper oil, served alongside Paul Carmichael's legendary buss up and shut roti. Caribbean fine dining at its most soulful.
ingredients: 1.5 lbs large shell-on shrimp (head-on if possible)
2 scotch bonnet peppers, seeded and minced
2 tbsp dried hibiscus flowers (flor de Jamaica)
instructions: Make hibiscus oil: steep dried hibiscus flowers in ½ cup warm neutral oil for 30 minutes. Strain and reserve the vivid red oil.
Season shrimp with allspice, smoked paprika, coriander, and salt. Let sit 15 minutes.
Heat 2 tbsp oil in a large skillet over high heat until smoking.
Sear shrimp in a single layer, 1.5 minutes per side until shells are charred. Remove and set aside.
Lower heat to medium. Add butter, garlic, ginger, and peppers. Cook 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add tomato paste and brown sugar. Stir and cook 1 minute.
Deglaze with lime juice. Return shrimp to pan, toss to coat.
Add thyme sprigs and scallions. Toss 30 seconds more.
Plate shrimp and drizzle generously with the hibiscus oil.
Serve with buss up and shut roti or warm flatbread.
pro_tips: Shell-on shrimp are essential — the shells char and hold sauce better, and you eat with your hands.
For the buss up and shut roti: make a simple paratha dough, cook on a hot tawa, then 'bust it up' by clapping it between your hands.
Dried hibiscus flowers are cheap at any Latin grocery — they give a gorgeous crimson color and tart floral flavor to the oil.
Adjust scotch bonnet to your heat tolerance, but this dish should have KICK.

signature_dish.name: Carbonara
signature_dish.description: Roscioli's Roman carbonara — silky egg sauce clinging to every strand of rigatoni, studded with crispy guanciale. No cream, no shortcuts, just the real thing done at the highest level. The dish that made this Roman import famous in NYC.
ingredients: 6 oz guanciale, cut into ½-inch batons
1.5 cups finely grated Pecorino Romano (about 4 oz)
instructions: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to boil.
In a cold skillet, arrange guanciale in a single layer. Turn heat to medium-low and render slowly until fat is translucent and meat is crispy (10-12 minutes). Do NOT rush this.
Remove guanciale to a plate. Reserve the rendered fat in the pan — you'll need about 3 tbsp.
In a bowl, whisk egg yolks and whole eggs with both cheeses and a very generous amount of black pepper until smooth and creamy.
Cook pasta 1 minute short of al dente. Reserve 2 cups pasta water before draining.
Add drained pasta to the skillet with guanciale fat over LOW heat. Toss to coat.
Remove pan completely from heat. Wait 30 seconds for it to cool slightly.
Pour egg and cheese mixture over the pasta. Toss vigorously, adding splashes of pasta water, until a glossy, creamy sauce forms that coats every piece.
Add guanciale back. Toss once more.
Serve immediately with extra Pecorino and black pepper on top.
pro_tips: The pan MUST be off direct heat when you add the egg mixture — too hot and you get scrambled eggs. The residual heat is enough.
Guanciale (cured pork jowl) is non-negotiable. Pancetta is a distant second. Bacon is not carbonara.
Roscioli's secret is the ratio: more yolks than whole eggs creates that impossibly rich, sticky sauce.
Save more pasta water than you think you need — it's the key to adjusting consistency.

signature_dish.name: Handmade Soba with Dashi
signature_dish.description: Muku's toothy, woodsy buckwheat soba noodles made with grains sourced from chef Manabu Asanuma's family farm in Yamagata Prefecture. Served with a rich dashi for dipping, this dish anchors the kaiseki experience at NYC's fastest Michelin-starred restaurant.
ingredients: For tsuyu dipping sauce: 1 cup dashi
Toppings: grated daikon, wasabi, sliced scallions, toasted nori
instructions: Make dashi: soak kombu in 4 cups cold water for 30 minutes. Bring to a gentle simmer (do NOT boil). Remove kombu just before boiling.
Add bonito flakes. Simmer 30 seconds, then turn off heat. Let steep 10 minutes. Strain through cheesecloth without pressing.
Make tsuyu: combine 1 cup dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Bring to a brief boil, then cool completely. Chill.
For soba: mix buckwheat and all-purpose flour. Make a well. Add water gradually, mixing with chopsticks until shaggy.
Knead dough firmly for 5-8 minutes until smooth and pliable. It will feel drier than wheat dough — that's correct.
Rest dough wrapped in plastic 30 minutes.
Roll out on a floured surface to 1-2mm thickness. Fold into thirds.
Cut into thin noodles (about 2mm wide) with a sharp knife. Dust with flour to prevent sticking.
Boil noodles in a large pot of unsalted water for 60-90 seconds. They cook fast.
Immediately drain and rinse under cold running water, rubbing gently to remove starch.
Serve noodles on a bamboo mat or plate with ice. Serve tsuyu in small cups alongside grated daikon, wasabi, scallions, and nori.
pro_tips: The 80/20 buckwheat-to-wheat ratio (ni-hachi soba) is the classic balance — enough gluten to hold together, enough buckwheat for flavor.
Stone-ground buckwheat flour has dramatically more flavor than regular. Japanese grocery stores or online specialty shops carry it.
Do NOT overcook — 60-90 seconds max. The noodles should have real bite (the 'toothy' quality Muku is known for).
Cold soba must be rinsed thoroughly — the starch rinse is what gives them that clean, distinct texture.

signature_dish.name: Ligurian Focaccia with Stracchino
signature_dish.description: Borgo's signature focaccia — impossibly thin, golden, and fragrant with rosemary and fruity olive oil, hiding a layer of soft, slightly salted stracchino cheese underneath its lightly charred layers. Andrew Tarlow's first Manhattan restaurant made this the must-order starter on every table.
ingredients: 4 tbsp extra-virgin olive oil (plus more for the pan)
200g stracchino cheese (or crescenza — if unavailable, use robiola or taleggio)
instructions: Dissolve yeast in warm water. Add flour and salt. Mix until shaggy, then knead 8-10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Add 2 tbsp olive oil, knead in until incorporated. Dough should be soft and slightly tacky.
Place in an oiled bowl, cover. Rise 1.5 hours until doubled.
Generously oil a half sheet pan (13x18). Turn dough out onto pan.
With oiled fingers, gently stretch dough to fill the pan. If it resists, rest 10 minutes and try again.
Dimple the surface deeply with your fingertips. Drizzle with remaining olive oil.
Press rosemary leaves into the dimples. Sprinkle with flaky salt.
Let rise again 30-40 minutes until puffy.
Preheat oven to 475°F. Bake 18-22 minutes until deeply golden and crispy on bottom.
Remove from oven. While still hot, slice horizontally through the middle to create two layers.
Spread stracchino generously on the bottom half. Replace top.
Cut into squares and serve immediately while the cheese is melting.
pro_tips: The olive oil in the pan is what gives focaccia di Recco its signature crispy, almost fried bottom — don't be shy with it.
Stracchino (also labeled crescenza) is the authentic choice — it's a creamy, tangy fresh cheese that melts beautifully. Italian delis and cheese shops carry it.
Stretch the dough THIN — Ligurian focaccia is nothing like thick Sicilian-style. Think cracker-thin in spots.
If you can't find stracchino, a mix of cream cheese and mozzarella (2:1 ratio) approximates the texture.

signature_dish.name: Wood-Fired Duck Carnitas Taco
signature_dish.description: Oxomoco's showstopper — Moulard duck leg slow-cooked in its own fat until falling apart, then crisped in their wood-burning oven. Nestled in a house-made corn tortilla with habanero salsa, pickled onions, and fresh herbs. The dish that earned chef Justin Bazdarich a Michelin star.
ingredients: 2 cups rendered duck fat or lard
1 red onion, thinly sliced (for pickled onions)
Fresh cilantro and radishes for garnish
instructions: Season duck legs with salt, brown sugar, pepper, cumin, and oregano. Refrigerate uncovered overnight (or at least 4 hours).
Preheat oven to 300°F. Place duck legs in a snug baking dish with garlic and bay leaves. Cover with melted duck fat or lard.
Cover tightly with foil. Cook 3 hours until meat is completely tender and pulling from the bone.
Meanwhile, make pickled onions: heat vinegar and sugar until dissolved. Pour over sliced red onion. Let sit 1 hour.
Make habanero salsa: char tomatillos and white onion under the broiler until blistered (8 min). Blend with habanero, salt, and a splash of water.
Remove duck from fat. Shred meat, discarding bones and skin.
Heat a cast iron skillet or grill to high. Spread shredded duck in a single layer. Sear without moving until edges are deeply crispy (4-5 minutes). Flip and crisp the other side.
Warm tortillas on a dry comal or directly over flame until pliable with light char marks.
Build tacos: tortilla, crispy duck carnitas, habanero salsa, pickled onions, cilantro, and thinly sliced radishes.
pro_tips: Confit is the key — cooking low and slow in fat makes the duck impossibly tender before you crisp it. Don't skip the overnight cure.
Save the duck fat after confiting — it's liquid gold for roasting potatoes, frying eggs, or making more carnitas.
If you have access to a wood-fired oven or charcoal grill, crisp the duck there instead of a skillet for that signature Oxomoco smokiness.
Moulard duck legs are larger and richer than Pekin — find them at specialty butchers or online (D'Artagnan is a good source).

signature_dish.name: Pepper Soup with Goat Meat
signature_dish.description: Dept of Culture's fiery, aromatic Nigerian pepper soup — a spicy, thin broth brimming with tender goat meat, uziza leaves, and a complex blend of West African spices. Chef Ayo Balogun's north-central Nigerian heritage shines in every sip of this deeply soulful dish that anchors the tasting menu.
ingredients: 2 lbs bone-in goat meat, cut into chunks (substitute lamb shoulder if needed)
3 habanero or scotch bonnet peppers, whole
1 tbsp alligator pepper (mbongo spice) or grains of paradise, ground
2 tbsp uziza leaves (or substitute a mix of basil and watercress)
Scent leaves or Thai basil for garnish
instructions: Rinse goat meat thoroughly. Place in a large pot with one chopped onion, half the garlic, half the ginger, and seasoning cubes.
Add 6 cups water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer. Cook 1-1.5 hours until goat is tender (skim any foam that rises).
While meat cooks, blend the remaining onion, garlic, ginger, and habanero peppers into a rough paste.
In a separate pan, heat palm oil over medium heat. Fry the blended pepper paste for 8-10 minutes until the raw smell cooks off.
Add ground crayfish, alligator pepper, calabash nutmeg, and uda seeds to the paste. Stir and cook 2 more minutes.
Add the spiced pepper base to the pot of goat meat and broth. Stir well.
Simmer for 20-25 minutes so flavors meld. The broth should be thin and peppery, not thick like a stew.
Stir in uziza leaves (or basil/watercress substitute) in the final 2 minutes.
Taste and adjust salt and heat. Garnish with scent leaves or torn Thai basil.
Serve in deep bowls with the broth spooned generously over the meat.
pro_tips: Nigerian pepper soup is a BROTH, not a stew — it should be light-bodied but intensely flavorful and spicy. Don't thicken it.

The West African spice blend (alligator pepper, calabash nutmeg, uda) is what makes this authentic. Find them at African grocery stores or online (Amazon has Nigerian pepper soup spice mix as a shortcut).
Ground crayfish is a umami bomb unique to Nigerian cooking — it adds a savory depth you can't replicate with anything else. Don't skip it.
Goat on the bone is essential for the broth's body. Ask your butcher to cut it into small pieces so the bones release their flavor.
This soup is traditionally served as a starter and is also a famous Nigerian hangover cure.
